{"problem_standardized_math":"Explain the distinction between conditioning of a numerical problem and stability of an algorithm in the context of solving linear systems. In particular, for a square system $Ax=b$ with $A\\in\\mathbf{R}^{n\\times n}$, where perturbations $A\\to\\tilde A$ and $b\\to\\tilde b$ produce the perturbed solution $\\tilde x$ satisfying $\\tilde A\\tilde x=\\tilde b$, use the approximation\n\\[\n\\frac{\\|x-\\tilde{x}\\|}{\\|x\\|}\n\\approx\n\\kappa(A)\\left[\n\\frac{\\|A-\\tilde{A}\\|}{\\|A\\|}\n+\n\\frac{\\|b-\\tilde{b}\\|}{\\|b\\|}\n\\right]\n\\]\nto interpret why a large condition number $\\kappa(A)$ indicates that $Ax=b$ is ill conditioned, while a modest value indicates that it is well conditioned. Also explain why conditioning is a property of the problem rather than of the algorithm. Then discuss stability as a property of the algorithm, using the fact that Algorithm A.1 together with triangular substitution produces a computed solution $\\tilde x$ with relative error approximately\n\\[\n\\frac{\\|x-\\tilde{x}\\|}{\\|x\\|}\\approx \\kappa(A)\\frac{\\mathrm{growth}(A)}{\\|A\\|}\\,\\mathbf{u},\n\\]\nwhere $\\mathrm{growth}(A)$ denotes the largest element arising in $A$ during the execution of Algorithm A.1. Comment on the worst-case behavior $\\mathrm{growth}(A)/\\|A\\|\\approx 2^{n-1}$, on the instability of Gaussian elimination without pivoting for matrices such as\n\\[\nA=\\begin{bmatrix}0&1\\\\1&2\\end{bmatrix},\n\\]\nand on the stability of Cholesky factorization with triangular substitution when $A$ is symmetric positive definite. You may also refer to the illustrative ill-conditioned example in which the exact solution is $x=(1,1)^T$, but changing the first entry of the right-hand side from $2.00001$ to $2$ changes the solution to $x=(0,2)^T$."}
